claims.claim: Intermittent injection of 6.66 kg LN resulted in a maximum post-injection battery temperature of 69.7 ± 6.4°C and absorbed 441.2 ± 7.8 kJ of heat from the TR battery, compared to continuous injection of the same dose which resulted in 81.6 ± 7.9°C and absorbed 428.5 ± 9.7 kJ.
claims.quote: The maximum average post-injection temperature of the battery is 81.6 ± 7.9°C under continuous injection and 69.7 ± 6.4°C under intermittent injection, highlighting the superior inhibition effect of intermittent LN injection.

claims.claim: When LN injection dose was below 5 kg, it did not effectively inhibit thermal runaway, resulting in insufficient control over battery temperature.
claims.quote: we observed that when the LN injection is below 5 kg, it doesn't effectively inhibit TR, resulting in insufficient control over the battery temperature.

claims.claim: As LN injection dose increased from 5.72 kg to 8.02 kg under intermittent injection, the cooling rate of the TR battery increased from 0.609 ± 0.012 to 0.955 ± 0.019 and heat absorbed increased from 326.5 ± 6.5 kJ to approximately 1.62 times that value.
claims.quote: The cooling rate of the TR battery increased from 0.609 ± 0.012 to 0.955 ± 0.019 with intermittent injections ranging from 5.72 kg to 8.02 kg of LN.

claims.claim: At the anomalous warming stage (95°C battery temperature), injection of only 1.76 kg of LN prevented thermal runaway with a maximum post-injection temperature of 68.6°C, whereas at TR initiation (135°C), 6.66 kg of LN was required to achieve a maximum post-injection temperature of 72.5°C.
claims.quote: when the anomalous warming stage (95°C), 1.76 kg of LN is injected, resulting in a maximum battery post-injection temperature of 68.6°C. As the battery begins to TR (135°C), 6.66 kg of LN is injected, yielding a maximum post-injection temperature of 72.5°C.

claims.claim: When the same amount of LN is injected at higher battery temperatures (135°C vs 320°C at TR onset), the total heat absorbed by LN remains almost unchanged, but the maximum post-injection battery temperature rises and the cooling rate decreases.
claims.quote: when the same amount of LN is injected, the total heat absorbed by the LN remains almost unchanged as the battery temperature increases. However, the maximum post-injection temperature of the battery rises, and the cooling rate of the battery decreases.

claims.claim: The SEI layer on the anode surface first decomposes at a temperature roughly above 90 °C, leading to exposure of the lithiated anode which then reacts with organic solvents of the liquid electrolyte, generating flammable hydrocarbon gases such as methane and ethane.
claims.quote: the SEI layer on the anode surface first decomposes at a temperature roughly above 90 °C. The decomposition of SEI leads to the exposure of the lithiated anode (or lithium metal). The exposed anode then reacts with the organic solvents of the liquid electrolyte, generating flammable hydrocarbon gases such as methane, ethane, and others

claims.claim: When cell temperature is above approximately 130 °C for PE or 160 °C for PP, the polyethylene or polypropylene separator begins to melt and shrink, causing an internal short circuit and further deterioration of the battery.
claims.quote: When the cell temperature is above ≈130 °C/160 °C, the polyethylene (PE)/polypropylene (PP) separator begins to melt and shrink, which causes an internal short circuit and further deterioration of the battery.

claims.claim: The lithium-transitional metal oxide cathode decomposes at a temperature of approximately 180 °C, releasing oxygen.
claims.quote: the lithium-transitional metal oxide cathode decomposes at a temperature of ≈180 °C, releasing oxygen.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway stage 3, the maximum battery temperature can reach as high as 1000 °C.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature can reach as high as 1000 °C.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in one battery can easily propagate to adjacent batteries in battery packs or modules due to their close vicinity and a relatively adiabatic environment, causing devastating failure of the entire system.
claims.quote: TR in one battery can easily propagate to the adjacent batteries in battery packs or modules, due to their close vicinity and a relatively adiabatic environment, causing the devastating failure of the entire system.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway can raise lithium-ion battery cell temperature to over 800 °C in a few seconds under mechanical stress, electrical overcharging, or external thermal conditions.
claims.quote: LIBs are prone to thermal runaway (TR) under mechanical stress, electrical overcharging, or external thermal conditions, resulting in a fast and self-propagating reaction that can raise the cell temperature to over 800 °C in few seconds

claims.claim: In confined spaces, thermal runaway consequences are significantly more severe than in open environments due to restricted heat dissipation and enhanced gas-phase reactions that exacerbate flame propagation and pressure accumulation.
claims.quote: Prior research indicates that the repercussions of TR are significantly more severe in confined areas than in open environments, attributable to restricted heat dissipation and enhanced gas-phase reactions that exacerbate flame propagation and pressure accumulation

claims.claim: Ultrafine water mist (UWM) is defined by droplet diameters of approximately 10 µm.
claims.quote: ultrafine water mist (UWM) – defined by droplet diameters of approximately 10 µm

claims.claim: Due to their comparatively low momentum, ultrafine droplets exhibit restricted penetration and distribution in high-temperature regions.
claims.quote: due to their comparatively low momentum, ultrafine droplets exhibit restricted penetration and distribution in high-temperature regions

claims.claim: The volume-based mean diameter (D32) of the UWM was 12.314 µm, while that of the conventional fine water mist was 119.043 µm.
claims.quote: The UWM's volume-based mean diameter (D32) was measured at 12.314 μm, while that of the fine water mist was 119.043 μm
